findings.title: epics findAll and milestones findAll return all records regardless of project membership
findings.description: GET /epics and GET /milestones have no access-scope filter. Any authenticated user holding the quasi-universal epics:read / milestones:read permission (all roles down to OBSERVATEUR and CONTRIBUTEUR) can enumerate ALL epics and milestones across ALL projects, including those they are not a member of. The parallel routes GET /projects/:id and individual findOne paths do enforce membership via assertProjectMembership / assertCanAccessProject, but the list endpoints simply have no such filter. The milestones findAll is identical (apps/api/src/milestones/milestones.service.ts lines 53-88).
findings.root_cause: The findAll methods in epics and milestones services never check project membership or inject a caller-scoped WHERE clause, unlike the projects service which uses AccessScopeService.
findings.impact: A low-privilege user (OBSERVATEUR, CONTRIBUTEUR) can harvest the names, descriptions, priorities, and related project IDs of every epic and milestone in the system by iterating GET /epics?page=N and GET /milestones?page=N. This leaks internal project planning information to users who were explicitly excluded from those projects.
findings.suggested_fix: Inject the caller's user ID and role into findAll, then apply the same membership-scoped WHERE clause used in ProjectsService.findAll: join through project.members where userId=callerId unless the caller holds projects:manage_any. Example: `where.project = { members: { some: { userId: callerId } } }` for non-privileged callers.
acceptance_criteria: GET /epics without projectId as OBSERVATEUR returns only epics whose parent project has the caller as a member
GET /milestones without projectId as CONTRIBUTEUR returns only milestones whose parent project has the caller as a member
ADMIN and RESPONSABLE (with projects:manage_any) still receive all records
findings.notes: Same issue exists in milestones service at apps/api/src/milestones/milestones.service.ts:53-88

findings.title: POST /milestones/:id/complete lacks project-membership check
findings.description: The `complete` endpoint marks any milestone as COMPLETED without verifying the caller is a member of the milestone's parent project. The controller handler (milestones.controller.ts:116) passes only the milestone ID to milestonesService.complete; no currentUserId or currentUserRole is passed, and the service performs no membership assertion. By contrast, the sibling methods update() and remove() call assertProjectMembership. Any user with the milestones:update permission (quasi-universal: all roles except OBSERVATEUR) can complete a milestone in any project.
findings.root_cause: The complete() service method was added or refactored without applying the same assertProjectMembership guard that update() and remove() use.
findings.impact: Any user with milestones:update can mark milestones they have no business accessing as completed, corrupting project progress tracking across the organization.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the controller to pass currentUserId and currentUserRole to milestonesService.complete, and add the assertProjectMembership call at the start of that method, mirroring update() and remove().
acceptance_criteria: A user who is not a member of the parent project receives 403 when calling POST /milestones/:id/complete
A member of the project (or a user with projects:manage_any) can complete the milestone

findings.title: hardDelete lacks defense-in-depth ownership assertion present in all sibling mutation methods
findings.description: All other project mutation methods (update, remove, archive, unarchive, updateMember, removeMember) open with an `await this.assertProjectOwnershipOrBypass(id, user)` call as defense-in-depth. The hardDelete method is the sole exception — it relies exclusively on the OwnershipGuard decorator in the controller. If the guard is bypassed (e.g., a future refactor adds an internal call path, or the guard registration order changes), the service layer provides no backstop. This is an inconsistency in a system that has explicitly documented (see comment BUG-04 / BUG-08 SEC-06) the intentional double-enforcement pattern.
findings.root_cause: The assertProjectOwnershipOrBypass call was omitted when hardDelete was written, breaking the project-wide defense-in-depth contract.
findings.impact: If the OwnershipGuard is bypassed (internal service-to-service call, guard ordering bug, test harness), an unprivileged user could permanently delete a project without ownership verification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await this.assertProjectOwnershipOrBypass(id, user);` as the first line of hardDelete(), before the findUnique call, mirroring the pattern in remove() and archive().
acceptance_criteria: hardDelete throws ForbiddenException when called with a user who is neither owner nor holder of projects:manage_any, even if the OwnershipGuard is bypassed
Existing unit tests for hardDelete still pass

findings.title: GET /projects/:id/snapshots uses @Param('id') without ParseUUIDPipe
findings.description: Every other route in ProjectsController uses `@Param('id', ParseUUIDPipe)` to validate and sanitize the resource ID. The getSnapshots handler at line 187 uses `@Param('id')` with no pipe. The raw string is then forwarded to projectsService.getSnapshots and used in a Prisma findMany `where: { projectId }`. While Prisma parameterizes values (no SQL injection), an attacker can probe for non-404 responses with arbitrary strings, and the missing UUID validation allows bypass of route-level input contract.
findings.root_cause: ParseUUIDPipe was accidentally omitted from the @Param('id') decorator on the getSnapshots handler.
findings.impact: Malformed / non-UUID project IDs are accepted and forwarded to the database layer. Combined with an invalid UUID, PostgreSQL will return 0 rows (uuid_generate_v4 cast failure is caught by Prisma and returns empty array), but the pattern breaks the uniform input validation contract and creates a discrepancy between documented and actual behaviour.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `@Param('id')` to `@Param('id', ParseUUIDPipe)` on line 187, mirroring all other handlers in the same controller.
acceptance_criteria: GET /projects/not-a-uuid/snapshots returns 400 Bad Request
GET /projects/<valid-uuid>/snapshots continues to return 200 with results

findings.title: Snapshot date filter params (from/to) are not validated and passed directly to new Date()
findings.description: The `from` and `to` query parameters for GET /projects/:id/snapshots are accepted as raw strings in the controller and forwarded to the service without any validation. The service passes them directly to `new Date()`. If an invalid date string is supplied (e.g., `from=not-a-date`), `new Date('not-a-date')` produces an Invalid Date object. Prisma will propagate this as an invalid timestamp to PostgreSQL, likely resulting in a 500 Internal Server Error rather than a 400 Bad Request, leaking stack trace details.
findings.root_cause: No @IsDateString validation is applied to the from/to query parameters before they reach the service.
findings.impact: Supplying invalid date strings triggers unhandled Invalid Date propagation which may result in a 500 response that leaks server internals. An attacker can probe the error response for framework and query structure details.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a DTO class with `@IsDateString() @IsOptional() from?: string` and `@IsDateString() @IsOptional() to?: string` and apply `@Query() query: SnapshotFilterDto` in the controller. Alternatively add explicit isNaN checks in the service before using the date values.
acceptance_criteria: GET /projects/:id/snapshots?from=not-a-date returns 400 Bad Request
GET /projects/:id/snapshots?from=2025-01-01&to=2026-01-01 continues to work correctly

findings.title: description fields lack @MaxLength on CreateProjectDto, CreateEpicDto, CreateMilestoneDto
findings.description: The `description` field in CreateProjectDto, CreateEpicDto (line 24-27), and CreateMilestoneDto (line 20-23) is decorated with @IsString @IsOptional but has no @MaxLength constraint. An attacker can submit a multi-megabyte string as a description, which will be stored verbatim in the database and returned in API responses. The global body size limit (bodyLimit in main.ts / Fastify config) is the only backstop. The name fields in the same DTOs correctly have @MaxLength. Same issue in ImportMilestoneDto (name, description, dueDate all lack @MaxLength/@IsDateString).
findings.root_cause: MaxLength constraints were applied to name fields but not to free-text description fields.
findings.impact: Oversized descriptions may cause excessive memory allocation during serialization and inflate stored data. Low severity given the global body limit, but breaks input contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(2000)` (or appropriate limit) to description fields in CreateProjectDto, CreateEpicDto, CreateMilestoneDto. For ImportMilestoneDto, add @MaxLength(200) on name, @MaxLength(2000) on description, and change dueDate from @IsString to @IsDateString.
acceptance_criteria: POST /projects with description.length > 2000 returns 400 Bad Request
POST /milestones/project/:id/import with oversized name returns 400 Bad Request
findings.notes: Also affects ImportMilestoneDto where dueDate uses @IsString instead of @IsDateString, bypassing date format enforcement

findings.title: managerId and sponsorId in CreateProjectDto use @IsString instead of @IsUUID
findings.description: managerId and sponsorId are used as UUID foreign keys to the users table, but are validated only as @IsString. Prisma will accept any string and then fail at the DB level with a foreign-key or UUID cast error if a non-UUID is supplied, returning a 500 instead of a 400. The AddMemberDto correctly uses @IsUUID() for userId. Inconsistent validation contract.
findings.root_cause: Copy/paste inconsistency: userId in AddMemberDto has @IsUUID, but manager/sponsor IDs in CreateProjectDto do not.
findings.impact: Supplying a non-UUID managerId causes an unhandled Prisma error (P2023 or invalid UUID format) which propagates as a 500 Internal Server Error rather than a clean 400.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `@IsString()` with `@IsUUID()` for both managerId and sponsorId in CreateProjectDto.
acceptance_criteria: POST /projects with managerId='not-a-uuid' returns 400 Bad Request
POST /projects with valid UUID managerId continues to work

findings.title: visibleStatuses in UpdateProjectDto uses @IsString (not @IsEnum) allowing arbitrary status strings
findings.description: The sibling field hiddenStatuses uses `@IsEnum(TaskStatus, { each: true })` to enforce valid enum values. visibleStatuses uses only `@IsString({ each: true })`, allowing any arbitrary string to be stored. These values are stored as a JSON array in the project row and later used to filter Kanban columns. Injecting unknown status codes could cause front-end rendering errors or bypass intended visibility restrictions.
findings.root_cause: hiddenStatuses received enum validation but visibleStatuses was left with the weaker @IsString constraint.
findings.impact: An attacker can store arbitrary strings in visibleStatuses, potentially causing front-end errors when the Kanban board tries to match task statuses against the stored list, or confusing future business logic that reads this field.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `@IsString({ each: true })` with `@IsEnum(TaskStatus, { each: true })` for visibleStatuses, mirroring hiddenStatuses.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /projects/:id with visibleStatuses=['INVALID_STATUS'] returns 400 Bad Request
PATCH /projects/:id with visibleStatuses=['IN_PROGRESS','TODO'] succeeds

findings.title: Milestone bulk import array has no @ArrayMaxSize — DoS via oversized payload
findings.description: The importMilestones endpoint accepts an array of milestone objects with no upper bound on array size. The service processes them one by one in a for loop (milestones.service.ts:193-235), issuing a separate DB query per item for duplicate detection (findFirst) plus one create per item. A large array (e.g., 10,000 entries) causes a long-running transaction, excessive DB connections, and potential memory exhaustion. The global Fastify body size limit is the only backstop.
findings.root_cause: No @ArrayMaxSize decorator was applied to the milestones array in ImportMilestonesDto.
findings.impact: A user with milestones:create permission can trigger a DoS by submitting a large milestones array, causing DB saturation and long-running HTTP requests.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@ArrayMaxSize(500)` (or a reasonable limit matching business needs) to the milestones field. Also refactor the import loop to use a single findMany for duplicate detection and a single createMany for inserts.
acceptance_criteria: POST /milestones/project/:id/import with milestones array length > 500 returns 400 Bad Request
Import with <= 500 entries continues to work

findings.title: projectId query filter in epics/milestones findAll not validated as UUID
findings.description: The projectId query parameter in GET /epics and GET /milestones is accepted as a raw string and passed directly to a Prisma where clause. While Prisma parameterizes the query (no SQL injection), an invalid UUID string will result in a Prisma P2023 error (Invalid value for database column type uuid) that propagates as a 500 rather than a 400. The same issue exists in milestones controller at line 82.
findings.root_cause: No ParseUUIDPipe or DTO-level @IsUUID validation is applied to the projectId query parameter.
findings.impact: Supplying a non-UUID projectId causes an unhandled Prisma error resulting in a 500 response instead of a clean 400. Leaks stack trace in non-production environments.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a custom pipe or a DTO with `@IsUUID() @IsOptional() projectId?: string` and use `@Query() query: EpicFilterDto` in the controller.
acceptance_criteria: GET /epics?projectId=not-a-uuid returns 400 Bad Request
GET /epics?projectId=<valid-uuid> continues to return filtered results
findings.notes: Same issue in milestones controller at apps/api/src/milestones/milestones.controller.ts:82

verified_clean: All controller routes have @RequirePermissions guard — no unguarded mutating routes
Global JwtAuthGuard is registered as APP_GUARD in auth.module.ts — all routes require authentication unless @Public
Global ValidationPipe has whitelist:true and forbidNonWhitelisted:true (main.ts:164-165) — extra properties stripped
All route params use ParseUUIDPipe EXCEPT getSnapshots :id (finding S2-4)
No $queryRawUnsafe or $executeRawUnsafe found in projects/epics/milestones services — all queries use Prisma structured API
No hardcoded secrets, tokens, or passwords in any scanned file
No user-supplied URL fields (no SSRF surface in these modules)
No file upload in these modules
CSV export filename is sanitized via regex replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9-_]/g, '_') before use in Content-Disposition header
Snapshot capture endpoint uses reports:export permission (not broadly available)
projects:manage_any bypass permission used consistently in OwnershipGuard and assertProjectOwnershipOrBypass
Import milestones dueDate validated via new Date() in service — invalid dates caught with isNaN check (milestones.service.ts:314-320) in validateImport path
AddMemberDto and UpdateMemberDto use @IsUUID for userId
clientIds array uses @IsString({ each: true }) with UUID validation via isUUID(t) in service (projects.service.ts:265-269)
All Prisma queries use parameterized values — no string concatenation SQL
JWT configuration reads secret from ConfigService (not hardcoded), with access TTL configured
Defense-in-depth ownership assertion implemented in update/remove/archive/unarchive/updateMember/removeMember (6 of 7 mutation methods)
